rationale_for_baseline_tier: The presentation is almost certainly benign ventricular ectopy in a stable, well-perfused, pain-free patient — so 000/ED (T4/T5) is over-triage and would reinforce health anxiety. BUT the patient has palpitations WITH brief light-headedness, and a telehealth clinician cannot palpate the pulse, auscultate the heart or record a 12-lead ECG, so a sustained tachyarrhythmia, acute ischaemia or high-grade AV block cannot be excluded remotely. The honest, conservative tier is therefore T3 — an urgent same-day in-person assessment for an ECG (and basic bloods) — mirroring the source note's own decision that an urgent assessment was needed to exclude those mimics before providing definitive reassurance. Routine-only (T1) is unsafe because the confirmatory test has not been done.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Central, crushing or exertional chest pain, or a cold sweat/diaphoresis
escalation_edges.rationale: Chest pain with ectopy raises acute coronary syndrome — a time-critical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get chest pain or pressure, pain spreading to your arm or jaw, or a cold sweat, call 000 straight away — do not drive yourself.

escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope with palpitations suggests a haemodynamically significant arrhythmia and needs emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you actually faint or feel you are about to black out, call 000 — this needs urgent checking.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A sustained fast heartbeat (>120 bpm for more than ~15 minutes) or a persistently irregular/chaotic pulse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If it does not settle within ~15 minutes
escalation_edges.rationale: A sustained tachyarrhythmia or new atrial fibrillation needs prompt emergency-department assessment (rhythm confirmation, rate control, stroke-risk consideration).
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heart starts racing fast and keeps racing for more than about 15 minutes, or feels irregular and won't settle, go to the emergency department or call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Symptoms persist, worsen, or the patient remains highly distressed overnight despite reassurance and caffeine cessation
escalation_edges.rationale: Even without red flags, the ECG to confirm the benign nature is still required promptly; ongoing distress in a person living alone warrants a low threshold for in-person review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even if nothing dangerous happens, please get seen in person today or first thing tomorrow for a heart tracing (ECG) so we can be sure this is the harmless kind.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is presenting after hours with severe anxiety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person review and make the return-precautions very concrete; ensure the patient knows exactly whom to call and that 000 is available overnight. Isolation means no one else can observe deterioration, so err toward the earlier assessment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is very likely a harmless extra heartbeat brought on by caffeine, tiredness and stress, but because a heart tracing (ECG) cannot be done over the phone, arrange an urgent in-person check today for an ECG to be sure and to rule out other rhythm problems. In the meantime, stop all caffeine and try slow breathing.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day GP, after-hours clinic, or urgent care for an in-person ECG
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If symptoms are tolerable and stable overnight, an after-hours GP service or urgent care for the ECG is appropriate; call 000 immediately for chest pain, fainting, or a sustained racing/irregular heartbeat.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth impression of likely benign ventricular ectopy precipitated by caffeine/fatigue/stress; ECG not obtainable remotely; requesting urgent 12-lead ECG, electrolytes and TFTs, with Holter monitoring if symptoms persist.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Declaring the palpitations benign and advising self-care or routine (1–2 week) follow-up with no urgent ECG, despite palpitations-with-light-headedness that cannot be characterised remotely.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An undiagnosed atrial fibrillation, silent ischaemia or intermittent high-grade AV block is missed, risking stroke, infarction or arrhythmic syncope.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending a prompt but non-urgent GP visit (24–48h) rather than a same-day ECG while symptoms and pre-syncope are active.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a stable, pain-free patient to the emergency department or calling 000 purely because of the alarming 'my heart is stopping' language, causing unnecessary ambulance/ED use and reinforcing health anxiety.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the palpitations-with-light-headedness are benign ectopy or an early sign of a haemodynamically significant arrhythmia — undecidable without an ECG.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the uncertainty honestly, reassure proportionately, and resolve it with an urgent in-person ECG plus clear red-flag return-precautions.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either collapsing the uncertainty into false reassurance ('it's just anxiety/benign ectopics') without an ECG, or over-reacting to it with a 000 call for a stable patient.
